pages.text:
. .
     .     .
      .      .
  .
 .
   here   was   a   wise   man,   a   great   artificer,   and   the   ord
conceived   love   for   him   and   received   him,   that   he   should
behold the uppermost dwellings and be an eye-witness of the
wise and great and inconceivable and immutable realm of od
lmighty, of the very wonderful and glorious and bright and
many-eyed   station   of   the   ord’s   servants,   and   of   the
inaccessible   throne   of   the   ord,   and   of   the   degrees   and
manifestations of the incorporeal hosts, and of the ineffable
ministration   of   the   multitude   of   the   elements,   and   of   the
various   apparition   and   inepressible   singing   of   the   host   of
herubim, and of the boundless light.
    t that time, he said, when my one hundred and sity-fifth
year was completed,  begat my son athusal.
    fter this too  lived two hundred years and completed of
all the years of my life three hundred and sity-five years.

n the first day of the month  was in my house alone and
was resting on my bed and slept.
    nd when  was asleep, great distress came up into my
heart, and  was weeping with my eyes in sleep, and  could not
understand what this distress was, or what would happen to
me.
    nd there appeared to me two men, eceeding big, so that
 never saw such on earth their faces were shining like the
sun, their eyes too   were   like a burning light, and from their lips
was fire coming forth with clothing and singing of various kinds
in   appearance   purple,   their   wings   were   brighter   than   gold,
their hands whiter than snow.
    hey were standing at the head of my bed and began to
call me by my name.
    nd  arose from my sleep and saw clearly those two men
standing in front of me.
    nd      saluted   them   and   was   seized   with   fear   and   the
appearance of my face was changed from terror, and those
men said to me
    ave courage, noch, do not fear the eternal od sent
us to you, and lo! ou shalt to-day ascend with us into heaven,
and you shall tell your sons and all your household all that they
shall do without you on earth in your house, and let no one
seek you till the ord return you to them.
    nd  made haste to obey them and went out from my
house,   and   made   to   the   doors,   as   it   was   ordered   me, and
summoned   my   sons   athusal   and   egim   and   aidad   and
made known to them all the marvels those   men   had told me.
  .
 .
   isten to me, my children,  know not whither  go, or what
will befall me now therefore, my children,  tell you turn not
from od before the face of the vain, who made not eaven
and earth, for these shall perish and those who worship them,
and may the ord make confident your hearts in the fear of
him. nd now, my children, let no one think to seek me, until
the ord return me to you.

.
 .
   t came to pass, when noch had told his sons, that the
ngels took him on to their wings and bore him up on to the
first heaven and placed him on the clouds. nd there  looked,
and again  looked higher, and saw the ether, and they placed
me   on   the   first   heaven   and   showed   me   a   very   great   ea,
greater than the earthly sea.
  .
 .
   hey brought before my face the elders and rulers of the
stellar orders, and showed me two hundred ngels, who rule
the stars and   their   services to the heavens, and fly with their
wings and come round all those who sail.
  .
 .
   nd here  looked down and saw the treasure-houses of
the snow, and the ngels who keep their terrible store-houses,
and the clouds whence they come out and into which they go.
  .
 .
   hey showed me the treasure-house of the dew, like oil of
the olive, and the appearance of its form, as of all the flowers
of   the   earth   further   many   ngels   guarding   the   treasure-
houses of these   things , and how they are made to shut and
open.
  .
 .
   nd those men took me and led me up on to the second
heaven,   and   showed   me   darkness,   greater   than   earthly
darkness,   and   there      saw   prisoners   hanging,   watched,
awaiting the great and boundless judgment, and these ngels
were   dark-looking,   more   than   earthly   darkness,   and
incessantly making weeping through all hours.
    nd  said to the men who were with me herefore are
these   incessantly   tortured   hey   answered   me   hese   are

od’s apostates, who obeyed not od’s commands, but took
counsel with their own will, and turned away with their prince,
who also   is   fastened on the fifth heaven.
    nd  felt great pity for them, and they saluted me, and
said to me an of od, pray for us to the ord and  answered
to   them   ho   am   ,   a   mortal   man,   that      should   pray   for
ngels ho knows whither  go, or what will befall me r
who will pray for me
  .
 .
   nd those men took me thence, and led me up on to the
third heaven, and placed me there and  looked downwards,
and saw the produce of these places, such as has never been
known for goodness.
    nd  saw all the sweet-flowering trees and beheld their
fruits, which were sweet-smelling, and all the foods borne   by
them   bubbling with fragrant ehalation.
    nd in the midst of the trees that of life, in that place
whereon the ord rests, when he goes up into paradise and
this tree is of ineffable goodness and fragrance, and adorned
more than every eisting thing and on all sides   it is   in form
gold-looking and vermilion and fire-like and covers all, and it
has produce from all fruits.
    ts root is in the garden at the earth’s end.
    nd paradise is between corruptibility and incorruptibility.
    nd two springs come out which send forth honey and
milk,   and   their   springs   send   forth   oil   and   wine,   and   they
separate into four parts, and go round with quiet course, and
go down into the   , between corruptibility
and incorruptibility.
    nd thence   they go forth along the earth, and have a
revolution to their circle even as other elements.
    nd here there is no unfruitful tree, and every place is
blessed.

nd   there are   three hundred ngels very bright, who keep
the garden, and with incessant sweet singing and never-silent
voices serve the ord throughout all days and hours.
    nd  said ow very sweet is this place, and those men
said to me
  .
 .
   his place,  noch, is prepared for the righteous, who
endure all manner of offence from those that easperate their
souls, who avert their eyes from iniquity, and make righteous
judgment,   and   give   bread   to   the   hungering,   and   cover   the
naked with clothing, and raise up the fallen, and help injured
orphans, and who walk without fault before the face of the
ord, and serve him alone, and for them is prepared this place
for eternal inheritance.
  .
 .
   nd those two men led me up on to the orthern side,
and showed me there a very terrible place, and   there were   all
manner   of   tortures   in   that   place   cruel   darkness   and
unillumined gloom, and there is no light there, but murky fire
constantly flaming aloft, and   there is   a fiery river coming forth,
and that whole place is everywhere fire, and everywhere   there
is   frost and ice, thirst and shivering, while the bonds are very
cruel,   and   the   ngels   fearful   and   merciless,   bearing   angry
weapons, merciless torture, and  said
    oe, woe, how very terrible is this place.
    nd   those   men   said   to   me   his   place,      noch,   is
prepared for those who dishonour od, who on earth practice
sin against nature, which is child-corruption after the sodomitic
fashion, magic-making, enchantments and devilish witchcrafts,
and who boast of their wicked deeds, stealing, lies, calumnies,
envy, rancour, fornication, murder, and who, accursed, steal
the souls of men, who, seeing the poor take away their goods
and themselves wa rich, injuring them for other men’s goods
who being able to satisfy the empty, made the hungering to
die being able to clothe, stripped the naked and who knew
not their creator, and bowed to the soulless   and lifeless   gods,
who   cannot   see   nor   hear,   vain   gods,   who   also   built   hewn

images and bow down to unclean handiwork, for all these is
prepared this place among these, for eternal inheritance.
  .
 .
   hose men took me, and led me up on to the fourth
heaven, and showed me all the successive goings, and all the
rays of the light of sun and moon.
    nd  measure their goings, and compared their light, and
saw that the sun’s light is greater than the moon’s.
    ts circle and the wheels on which it goes always, like the
wind going past with very marvellous speed, and day and night
it has no rest.
    ts passage and return   are accompanied by   four great
stars,   and   each star has under it a thousand stars, to the right
of the sun’s wheel,   and by   four to the left, each having under it
a thousand stars, altogether eight thousand, issuing with the
sun continually.
    nd by day fifteen myriads of ngels attend it, and by
night a thousand.
    nd si-winged ones issue with the ngels before the
sun’s wheel into the fiery flames, and a hundred ngels kindle
the sun and set it alight.
  .
 .
   nd  looked and saw other flying elements of the sun,
whose   names   are   hoenies   and   halkydri,   marvellous   and
wonderful,   with   feet   and   tails   in   the   form   of   a   lion,   and   a
crocodile’s   head,   their   appearance   is   empurpled,   like   the
rainbow their size   is   nine hundred measures, their wings   are
like   those of ngels, each   has   twelve, and they attend and
accompany the sun, bearing heat and dew, as it is ordered
them from od.
    hus   the   sun   revolves   and goes, and   rises   under the
heaven, and its course goes under the earth with the light of
its rays incessantly.

.
 .
   hose men bore me away to the east, and placed me at
the sun’s gates, where the sun goes forth according to the
regulation of the seasons and the circuit of the months of the
whole year, and the number of the hours day and night.
    nd  saw si gates open, each gate having sity-one
stadia and a quarter of one stadium, and  measured   them
 truly, and understood their size   to be   so much, through which
the sun goes forth, and goes to the west, and is made even,
and rises throughout all the months, and turns back again from
the si gates according to the succession of the seasons thus
 the period   of the whole year is finished after the returns of the
four seasons.
  .
 .
   nd again those men led me away to the western parts,
and   showed   me   si   great   gates   open   corresponding   to   the
eastern gates, opposite to where the sun sets, according to the
number of the days three hundred and sity-five and a quarter.
     hus again it goes down to the western gates,   and   draws
away its light, the greatness of its brightness, under the earth
for since the crown of its shining is in heaven with the ord,
and guarded by four hundred ngels, while the sun goes round
on wheel under the earth, and stands seven great hours in
night,   and   spends   half   its   course   under   the   earth,   when   it
comes to the eastern approach in the eighth hour of the night,
it   brings   its   lights,   and   the   crown   of   shining,   and   the   sun
flames forth more than fire.
  .
 .
   hen   the   elements   of   the   sun,   called   hoenies   and
halkydri break into song, therefore every bird flutters with its
wings, rejoicing at the giver of light, and they broke into song
at the command of the ord.
     he giver of light comes to give brightness to the whole
world, and the morning guard takes shape, which is the rays of
the sun, and the sun of the earth goes out, and receives its

brightness to light up the whole face of the earth, and they
showed me this calculation of the sun’s going.
    nd the gates which it enters, these are the great gates
of the calculation of the hours of the year for this reason the
sun is a great creation, whose circuit   lasts   twenty-eight years,
and begins again from the beginning.
  .
 .
   hose   men   showed   me   the   other   course,   that   of   the
moon,   twelve   great   gates,   crowned   from   west   to   east,   by
which the moon goes in and out of the customary times.
    t goes in at the first gate to the western places of the
sun,   by the   first gates   with   thirty -one   days   eactly, by   the
second gates with thirty-one days eactly, by the third with
thirty days eactly, by the fourth with thirty days eactly, by
the fifth with thirty-one days eactly, by the sith with thirty-
one days eactly, by the seventh with thirty days eactly, by
the   eighth   with thirty-one   days   perfectly,   by   the   ninth   with
thirty-one days eactly, by the tenth with thirty days perfectly,
by the eleventh with thirty-one days eactly, by the twelfth
with twenty-eight days eactly.
    nd it goes through the western gates in the order and
number of the eastern, and accomplishes the three hundred
and sity-five and a quarter days of the solar year, while the
lunar year has three hundred fifty-four, and there are wanting
 to it   twelve days of the solar circle, which are the lunar epacts
of the whole year.
    hus,   too,   the   great   circle   contains   five   hundred   and
thirty-two years.
    he quarter   of a day   is omitted for three years, the fourth
fulfils it eactly.
    herefore   they   are   taken   outside   of   heaven   for   three
years and are not added to the number of days, because they
change   the   time   of   the   years   to   two   new   months   towards
completion, to two others towards diminution.
    nd when the western gates are finished, it returns and
goes to the eastern to the lights, and goes thus day and night

about the heavenly circles, lower than all circles, swifter than
the   heavenly   winds,   and   spirits   and   elements   and   ngels
flying each ngel has si wings.
    t has a sevenfold course in nineteen years.
  .
 .
   n the midst of the heavens  saw armed soldiers, serving
the ord, with tympana and organs, with incessant voice, with
sweet   voice,   with   sweet   and   incessant   voice   and   various
singing,   which   it   is   impossible   to   describe,   and   which
 astonishes   every   mind,   so   wonderful   and   marvellous   is   the
singing of those ngels, and  was delighted listening to it.
  .
 .
   he men took me on to the fifth heaven and placed me,
and there  saw many and countless soldiers, called rigori, of
human appearance, and their size   was   greater than that of
great giants and their faces withered, and the silence of their
mouths   perpetual,   and   their   was   no   service   on   the   fifth
heaven, and  said to the men who were with me
    herefore   are   these   very   withered   and   their   faces
melancoly, and their mouths silent, and   wherefore   is there no
service on this heaven
    nd they said to me hese are the rigori, who with
their prince atanail rejected the ord of light, and after them
are   those   who   are   held   in   great   darkness   on   the   second
heaven, and three of them went down on to earth from the
ord’s throne, to the   place rmon, and broke through their
vows on the shoulder of the hill rmon and saw the daughters
of men how good they are, and took to themselves wives, and
befouled the earth with their deeds, who in all times of their
age made lawlessness and miing, and giants are born and
marvellous big men and great enmity.
    nd therefore od judged them with great judgment, and
they weep for their brethren and they will be punished on the
ord’s great day.

nd  said to the rigori  saw your brethren and their
works, and their great torments, and  prayed for them, but the
ord has condemned them   to be   under earth till   the eisting
 heaven and earth shall end for ever.
    nd  said herefore do you wait, brethren, and do not
serve before the ord’s face, and have not put your services
before the ord’s face, lest you anger your ord utterly
    nd they listened to my admonition, and spoke to the
four ranks in heaven, and lo! s  stood with those two men
four trumpets trumpeted together with great voice, and the
rigori broke into song with one voice, and their voice went up
before the ord pitifully and affectingly.
  .
 .
   nd thence those men took me and bore me up on to the
sith heaven, and there  saw seven bands of ngels, very
bright and very glorious, and their faces shining more than the
sun’s shining, glistening, and there is no difference in their
faces, or behaviour, or manner of dress and these make the
orders, and learn the goings of the stars, and the alteration of
the moon, or revolution of the sun, and the good government
of the world.
    nd when they see evildoing they make commandments
and instruction, and sweet and loud singing, and all   songs   of
praise.
    hese   are   the   rchangels   ()   who   are   above   ngels,
measure all life in heaven and on earth, and the ngels who
are   appointed   over seasons and years, the ngels who are
over rivers and sea, and who are over the fruits of the earth,
and the ngels who are over every grass, giving food to all, to
every living thing, and the ngels who write all the souls of
men, and all their deeds, and their lives before the ord’s face
in their midst are si hoenies and si herubim and si si-
winged ones continually with one voice singing one voice, and
it is not possible to describe their singing, and they rejoice
before the ord at his footstool.
  .
 .

nd those two men lifted me up thence on to the seventh
heaven, and  saw there a very great light, and fiery troops of
great rchangels, () incorporeal forces, and dominions, orders
and governments, cherubim and seraphim, thrones and many-
eyed ones, nine regiments, the oanit stations of light, and 
became afraid, and began to tremble with great terror, and
those men took me, and led me after them, and said to me
    ave courage, noch, do not fear, and showed me the
ord from afar, sitting on is very high throne. or what is
there on the tenth heaven, since the ord dwells there
    n the tenth heaven is od, in the ebrew tongue he is
called ravat ().
    nd all the heavenly troops would come and stand on the
ten steps according to their rank, and would bow down to the
ord, and would again go to their places in joy and felicity,
singing   songs   in   the   boundless   light   with   small   and   tender
voices, gloriously serving him.
  .
 .
   nd   the   cherubim   and   seraphim   standing   about   the
throne,   the   si-winged   and   many-eyed   ones   do   not   depart,
standing before the ord’s face doing his will, and cover his
whole throne, singing with gentle voice before the ord’s face
oly, oly, oly, ord uler of abaoth, heavens and earth are
full of our glory.
    hen      saw   all   these   things,   those   men   said   to   me
noch, thus far is it commanded us to journey with you, and
those men went away from me and thereupon  saw them not.
    nd  remained alone at the end of the seventh heaven
and became afraid, and fell on my face and said to myself
oe is me, what has befallen me
    nd the ord sent one of his glorious ones, the rchangel
() abriel, and   he   said to me ave courage, noch, do not
fear, arise before the ord’s face into eternity, arise, come with
me.
    nd  answered him, and said in myself y ord, my soul
is departed from me, from terror and trembling, and  called to

the men who led me up to this place, on them  relied, and   it is
 with them  go before the ord’s face.
    nd abriel caught me up, as a leaf caught up by the
wind, and placed me before the ord’s face.
    nd      saw   the   eighth   heaven,   which   is   called   in   the
ebrew tongue uzaloth, changer of the seasons, of drought,
and of wet, and of the twelve constellations of the circle of the
firmament, which are above the seventh heaven.
    nd  saw the ninth heaven, which is called in ebrew
uchavim,   where   are   the   heavenly   homes   of   the   twelve
constellations of the circle of the firmament.
  .
 .
   n the tenth heaven,   which is called   ravoth,  saw the
appearance of the ord’s face, like iron made to glow in fire,
and brought out, emitting sparks, and it burns.
    hus   in a moment of eternity    saw the ord’s face, but
the ord’s face is ineffable, marvellous and very awful, and
very, very terrible.
    nd who am  to tell of the ord’s unspeakable being, and
of his very wonderful face nd  cannot tell the quantity of his
many instructions, and various voices, the ord's throne   is   very
great   and   not   made   with   hands,   nor   the   quantity   of   those
standing   round   him,   troops   of   cherubim   and   seraphim,   nor
their incessant singing, nor his immutable beauty, and who
shall tell of the ineffable greatness of his glory.
    nd  fell prone and bowed down to the ord, and the
ord with his lips said to me
    ave courage, noch, do not fear, arise and stand before
my face into eternity.
    nd the archistratege () ichael lifted me up, and led
me to before the ord’s face.
    nd the ord said to his servants tempting them et
noch   stand   before   my   face   into   eternity,   and   the   glorious

ones   bowed   down   to   the   ord,   and   said   et   noch   go
according to our word.
    nd the ord said to ichael o and take noch from out
 of   his   earthly   garments,   and   anoint   him   with   my   sweet
ointment, and put him into the garments of y glory.
    nd ichael did thus, as the ord told him. e anointed
me, and dressed me, and the appearance of that ointment is
more than the great light, and his ointment is like sweet dew,
and its smell mild, shining like the sun’s ray, and  looked at
myself, and      was like one of his glorious ones ().
    nd the ord summoned one of his rchangels by name
ravuil,   whose   knowledge   was   quicker   in   wisdom   than   the
other rchangels, who wrote all the deeds of the ord and the
ord said to ravuil ring out the books from my store-houses,
and a reed of quick-writing, and give   it   to noch, and deliver to
him the choice and comforting books out of your hand.
  .
 .
   nd he was telling me all the works of heaven, earth and
sea, and all the elements, their passages and goings, and the
thunderings of the thunders, the sun and moon, the goings and
changes of the stars, the seasons, years, days, and hours, the
risings   of   the   wind,   the   numbers   of   the   ngels,   and   the
formation of their songs, and all human things, the tongue of
every human song and life, the commandments, instructions,
and sweet-voiced singings, and all things that it is fitting to
learn.
    nd ravuil told me ll the things that  have told you,
we   have   written.   it   and   write   all   the   souls   of   mankind,
however many of them are born, and the places prepared for
them to eternity for all souls are prepared to eternity, before
the formation of the world.
    nd all double thirty days and thirty nights, and  wrote
out all things eactly, and wrote three hundred and sity-si
books.
  .
 .

nd the ord summoned me, and said to me noch, sit
down on my left with abriel.
    nd  bowed down to the ord, and the ord spoke to me
noch, beloved, all   that   you see, all things that are standing
finished  tell to you even before the very beginning, all that 
created from non-being, and visible things from invisible.
    ear, noch, and take in these my words, for not to y
ngels have  told my secret, and  have not told them their
rise,   nor   my   endless   realm,   nor   have   they   understood   my
creating, which  tell you to-day.
    or   before   all   things   were   visible,      alone   used   to   go
about in the invisible things, like the sun from east to west,
and from west to east.
    ut even the sun has peace in itself, while  found no
peace, because  was creating all things, and  conceived the
thought of placing foundations, and of creating visible creation.
  .
 .
    commanded in the very lowest   parts , that visible things
should come down from invisible, and doil () came down
very great, and  beheld him, and lo! e had a belly of great
light.
    nd  said to him ecome undone, doil, and let the
visible   come   out of you.
    nd he came undone, and a great light came out. nd 
 was   in the midst of the great light, and as there is born light
from   light,   there   came   forth   a   great   age,   and   showed   all
creation, which  had thought to create.
    nd  saw that   it was   good.
    nd  placed for myself a throne, and took my seat on it,
and said to the light o thence up higher and fi yourself high
above the throne, and be a foundation to the highest things.
    nd above the light there is nothing else, and then  bent
up and looked up from my throne.

.
 .
   nd  summoned the very lowest a second time, and said
et rchas () come forth hard, and he came forth hard from
the invisible.
    nd rchas came forth, hard, heavy, and very red.
    nd  said e opened, rchas, and let there be born from
you, and he came undone, an age came forth, very great and
very dark, bearing the creation of all lower things, and  saw
that   it was   good and said to him
    o thence down below, and make yourself firm, and be a
foundation for the lower things, and it happened and he went
down and fied himself, and became the foundation for the
lower things, and below the darkness there is nothing else.
  .
 .
   nd  commanded that there should be taken from light
and darkness, and  said e thick, and it became thus, and 
spread it out with the light, and it became water, and  spread
it out over the darkness, below the light, and then  made firm
the   waters,   that   is   to   say   the   bottomless,   and      made
foundation of light around the water, and created seven circles
from inside, and imaged   the water   like crystal wet and dry,
that is to say like glass,   and   the circumcision of the waters and
the other elements, and  showed each one of them its road,
and the seven stars each one of them in its heaven, that they
go thus, and  saw that it was good.
    nd  separated between light and between darkness,
that is to say in the midst of the water hither and thither, and 
said to the light, that it should be the day, and to the darkness,
that it should be the night, and there was evening and there
was morning the first day.
  .
 .
   nd then  made firm the heavenly circle, and   made   that
the lower water which is under heaven collect itself together,

into one whole, and that the chaos become dry, and it became
so.
    ut of the waves  created rock hard and big, and from
the rock  piled up the dry, and the dry  called earth, and the
midst of the earth  called abyss, that is to say the bottomless,
 collected the sea in one place and bound it together with a
yoke.
    nd  said to the sea ehold  give you   your   eternal
limits, and   you shalt not break loose   from your component
parts.
    hus  made fast the firmament. his day  called the
first-created.
  .
 .
   nd for all the heavenly troops  imaged the image and
essence of fire, and my eye looked at the very hard, firm rock,
and   from   the   gleam   of   my   eye   the   lightning   received   its
wonderful nature,   which   is both fire in water and water in fire,
and one does not put out the other, nor does the one dry up
the   other,   therefore   the   lightning   is   brighter   than   the   sun,
softer than water and firmer than hard rock.

will turn away his face from the labour of his hand, and   that
 man cannot find the labour of his hands.
    nd if his hands made it, but his heart murmur, and his
heart cease not making murmur incessantly, he has not any
advantage.
  .
 .
   lessed is the man who in his patience brings his gifts
with   faith   before   the   ord’s   face,   because   he   will   find
forgiveness of sins.

ut if he take back his words before the time, there is no
repentance for him and if the time pass and he do not of his
own will what is promised, there is no repentance after death.
    ecause every work which man does before the time, is
all deceit before men, and sin before od.
  .
 .
   hen man clothes the naked and fills the hungry, he will
find reward from od.
    ut if his heart murmur, he commits a double evil ruin of
himself and of that which he gives and for him there will be no
finding of reward on account of that.
    nd if his own heart is filled with his food and his own
flesh, clothed with his own clothing, he commits contempt, and
will forfeit all his endurance of poverty, and will not find reward
of his good deeds.
    very   proud   and   magniloquent   man   is   hateful   to   the
ord, and every false speech, clothed in untruth it will be cut
with the blade of the sword of death, and thrown into the fire,
and shall burn for all time.
  .
 .
   hen   noch   had   spoken   these   words   to   his   sons,   all
people far and near heard how the ord was calling noch.
hey took counsel together
    et us go and kiss noch, and two thousand men came
together and came to the place chuzan where noch was,
and his sons.
    nd the elders of the people, the whole assembly, came
and bowed down and began to kiss noch and said to him
    ur father noch,   may   you   be   blessed of the ord, the
eternal ruler, and now bless your sons and all the people, that
we may be glorified to-day before your face.

or you shalt be glorified before the ord’s face for all
time, since the ord chose you, rather than all men on earth,
and   designated   you   writer   of   all   his   creation,   visible   and
invisible, and redeemed of the sins of man, and helper of your
household.
  .
 .
   nd   noch   answered   all   his   people   saying   ear,   my
children,   before   that   all   creatures   were   created,   the   ord
created the visible and invisible things.
    nd   as   much   time   as   there   was   and   went   past,
understand that after all that he created man in the likeness of
his own form, and put into him eyes to see, and ears to hear,
and heart to reflect, and intellect wherewith to deliberate.
    nd the ord saw all man’s works, and created all his
creatures, and divided time, from time he fied the years, and
from the years he appointed the months, and from the months
he appointed the days, and of days he appointed seven.
    nd in those he appointed the hours, measured them out
eactly,   that   man   might   reflect   on   time   and   count   years,
months, and hours,   their   alternation, beginning, and end, and
that   he   might   count   his   own   life,   from   the   beginning   until
death, and reflect on his sin and write his work bad and good
because no work is hidden before the ord, that every man
might   know   his   works   and   never   transgress   all   his
commandments, and keep my handwriting from generation to
generation.
    hen   all   creation   visible   and   invisible,   as   the   ord
created   it,   shall   end,   then   every   man   goes   to   the   great
judgment, and then all time shall perish, and the years, and
thenceforward there will be neither months nor days nor hours,
they will be adhered together and will not be counted.
    here will be one aeon, and all the righteous who shall
escape the ord’s great judgment, shall be collected in the
great aeon, for the righteous the great aeon will begin, and
they will live eternally, and then too there will be amongst
them neither labour, nor sickness, nor humiliation, nor aniety,
nor need, nor brutality, nor night, nor darkness, but great light.

nd they shall have a great indestructible wall, and a
paradise bright and incorruptible, for all corruptible things shall
pass away, and there will be eternal life.
  .
 .
   nd now, my children, keep your souls from all injustice,
such as the ord hates.
    alk before his face with terror and trembling and serve
him alone.
    ow down to the true od, not to dumb idols, but bow

    or the ord sees all things when man takes thought in

    f   you   look   to   heaven,   the   ord   is   there   if   you   take

    or the ord created all things. ow not down to things

 reliance   on   promises,   in   illness,   in   abuse,   in   wounds,   in
temptation, in nakedness, in privation, loving one another, till

    lessed   are   the   just   who   shall   escape   the   great

hen noch had talked to the people, the ord sent out
darkness   on   to   the   earth,   and   there   was   darkness,   and   it
covered those men standing with noch, and they took noch
up   on   to   the   highest   heaven,   where   the   ord   is    and   he
received him and placed him before his face, and the darkness

   noch was born on the sith day of the month sivan,
and lived three hundred and sity-five years.
    e was taken up to heaven on the first day of the month
sivan and remained in heaven sity days.
    e wrote all these signs of all creation, which the ord

handed them over to his sons and remained on earth thirty
days, and was again taken up to heaven on the sith day of
the month sivan, on the very day and hour when he was born.
    s every man’s nature in this life is dark, so are also his

and at that hour too he died.
    ethosalam   and   his   brethren,   all   the   sons   of   noch,
